initiative_name: Chapter 6: Adapting to the effects of climate change (Adaptation) > 2. Direction and structure of measures
parent_statement: Hiroshima City will implement an Adaptation Plan with two pillars: improving acknowledgement/understanding of climate change and improving regional comprehensive ability to deal with climate risks, and will comprehensively develop three measures under these pillars.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively align with the parent’s two pillars by pairing awareness-raising (Sub-action 1) with multiple risk-focused measures that build regional capacity (Subs 2–5) and an enabling monitoring/evaluation system (Sub-action 6). A key gap is the absence of specified performance levels (how much risk reduction/coverage) and any deadlines, which makes it hard to judge whether the measures are sufficient to meet the plan’s intended ambition.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary: they move from understanding/communication to integrating adaptation into existing domains, then implement hazard-specific measures (heavy rain, heat health, heat island), and finally add a learning/evaluation system. There is some overlap between the heat-health messaging (Sub-action 4) and broader awareness efforts (Sub-action 1), but it is not contradictory and can be seen as audience-specific communication.
weak_signals: No numeric targets, no deadlines/years, promote/publicity/education language, consider a system (process-only), steady promotion without measurable endpoints
strong_signals: Concrete deliverables mentioned (disaster information sharing system, enhancement of disaster information delivery, city renovation for disaster prevention, roof/wall greening, conservation/creation of public open space), cooperation with state and universities for evaluation system
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to implement Hiroshima City’s adaptation approach under two pillars—improving climate-change understanding and improving regional capacity to manage climate risks—via a package of awareness, sectoral measures (heavy rain, health, heat island), and a learning/evaluation system. 2) Strength mix across 6 sub-actions: 0 strong, 2 moderate (Actions 3 and 5 are tangible interventions), and 4 weak (Actions 1, 2, 4, 6 are largely promotional or process/coordination without measurable endpoints). 3) The parent header provides organizational structure (pillars/measures) but does not provide a binding mechanism (e.g., ordinance) or explicit spatial scope (e.g., citywide), so weaker subs are not lifted by inherited enforceability/scope. 4) Coverage is fairly good because the subs address both pillars and multiple key climate risks, but measurability is weak because the cluster contains no explicit quantitative outcome targets and no timelines anywhere in the verbatim text; coherence is high because the elements fit together as an implementation pathway (awareness + delivery actions + evaluation). 5) Applying the soundness rule: it cannot be SOUND because aggregate_measurability is 'No' and there is no 'strong' sub-action; it qualifies as PARTIAL because coverage_score >= 0.4, at least one sub-action is moderate, and the cluster includes concrete, non-aspirational interventions (e.g., building/constructing/enhancing systems and greening), even though it lacks targets and deadlines.
